audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear an interview with a woman who is doing a survey on transport.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 5.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Excuse me, would you mind if I asked you some questions?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're doing a survey on transport.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: First of all can I take your name?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it's Sadie Jones.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Thanks very much.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And could I have your date of birth?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Just the year will do, actually.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that all right?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. Yes, that's fine.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: So, next your post code please.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's DW307YZ.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Great, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Is that in Wells?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, it's actually in Harborne.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Wells isn't far from there, though.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: I really like that area.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: My grandmother lived there when I was a kid.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it is nice.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Right, so now I want to ask you some questions about how you traveled here today.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Did you use public transport?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I came by bus.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: OK, and that was today.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's the 24th of April, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Isn't it the 25th uh, no, ha, actually you're right.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Ha ha ha.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what was the reason for your trip today?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I can see you've got some shopping with you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I did some shopping, but the main reason I came here was to go to the dentist.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: That's not much fun.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hope it was nothing serious.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, it was just a check-up.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: It's fine.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you normally travel by bus into the city center?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I stopped driving ages ago, because parking was so difficult to find, and it cost so much.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: The bus is much more convenient too. It only takes about 30 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: That's good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So where did you start your journey?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: At the bus stop on Claxby Street.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Is that CLAXBY?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 6 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And how satisfied with the service are you?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you have any complaints?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, as I said, it's very convenient and quick when it's on time.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: But this morning it was late, only about 10 minutes, but still.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Yes, I understand that's annoying.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And what about the timetable?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you have any comments about that?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm, I suppose I mainly use the bus during the day, but anytime I've been in town in the evening.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Uh. For dinner or at the cinema.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: I've noticed you have to wait a long time for a bus.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Uh. There aren't that many.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: OK, thanks.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So now, I'd like to ask you about your car use.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I have got a car, but I don't use it that often.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: Mainly just to go to the supermarket.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: But that's about it really.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: My husband uses it at the weekends to go to the golf club.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: And what about a bicycle?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I don't actually have one at the moment.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: What about the city bikes you can rent?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Do you ever use those?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, I'm not keen on cycling there because of all the pollution.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: But I would like to get a bike.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: It would be good to use it to get to work.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: So, why haven't you got one now?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I live in a flat on the 2nd floor.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: And it doesn't have any storage, so we'd have to leave it in the hall outside the flat.
audioscript.speaker: SADIE

audioscript.text: I see.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, well I think that's all we need from you today...
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear a woman speaking to a group of people who are interested in becoming volunteers for an organization called ACE.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good evening everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Let me start by welcoming you all to this talk, and thanking you for taking the time to consider joining ACE voluntary organization.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: ACE offers support to people and services in the local area, and we're now looking for more volunteers to help us do this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: By the way, I hope you're all comfortable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have brought in extra seats so that no one has to stand, but it does mean that the people at the back of the room may be a bit squashed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We'll only be here for about half an hour, so, hopefully, that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of the first questions we're often asked is how old you need to be to volunteer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, you can be as young as 16, or you can be 60 or over.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It all depends on what type of voluntary work you want to do.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Other considerations such as reliability are crucial in voluntary work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And age isn't related to these in our experience.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another question we get asked relates to training.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, there's plenty of that, and it's all face to face.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What's more, training doesn't end when you start working for us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It takes place before, during, and after periods of work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Often it's run by other experienced volunteers, as managers tend to prefer to get on with other things.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, I would ask you to consider a couple of important issues before you decide to apply for voluntary work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We don't worry about why you want to be a volunteer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: People have many different reasons that range from getting work experience to just doing something they've always wanted to do, but it is critical that you have enough hours in the day.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For whatever role we agree is suitable for you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If being a volunteer becomes stressful, then it's best not to do it at all.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may think that your income is important.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we don't ask about that.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's up to you to decide if you can work without earning money.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: What we value is dedication.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of our most loyal volunteers earn very little themselves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But still give their full energy to the work they do with us.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, so let's take a look at some of the work areas that we need volunteers for, and the sort of things that would help you in those.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may wish simply to help us raise money.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you have the creativity to come up with an imaginative or novel way of fundraising, we'd be delighted.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As standing in the local streets or shops with a collection box can be rather boring.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One outdoor activity that we need volunteers for is litter collection, and for this it's useful if you can walk for long periods, sometimes uphill.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Some of our regular collectors are quite elderly, but very active and keen to protect the environment.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If you enjoy working with children, we have three vacancies for what are called playmates.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These volunteers help children learn about staying healthy.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Through a range of out of school activities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You don't need to have children yourself, but it's good if you know something about nutrition, and can give clear instructions.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If that doesn't appeal to you, maybe you would be interested in helping out at our story club for disabled children, especially if you have done some acting.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We put on three performances a year based on books they have read, and we're always looking for support with the theatrical side of this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The last area I'll mention today is first aid.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Volunteers who join this group can end up teaching others in vulnerable groups who may be at risk of injury.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Initially though, your priority will be to take in a lot of information, and not forget any important steps or details.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Right, so does anyone have any questions...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Why does the speaker apologise about the seats?
options: They are too small.
There are not enough of them.
Some of them are very close together.

items.title: What does the speaker say about the age of volunteers?
options: The age of volunteers is less important than other
Young volunteers are less reliable than older ones.
Most volunteers are about 60 years old.

items.title: What does the speaker say about training?
options: It is continuous.
It is conducted by a manager.
It takes place online.

body.title: Which TWO issues does the speaker ask the audience to consider before they apply to be volunteers?
options: their financial situation
their level of commitment
their work experience
their ambition
their availability

text: What does the speaker suggest would be helpful for each of the following areas of voluntary work?
textReadable: What does the speaker suggest would be helpful for each of the following areas of voluntary work?

list: an understanding of food and diet
body.title: Area of voluntary work

test_text.title: Talk on jobs in fashion design

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two students called Hugo and Chantal discussing a talk they have just attended at the start of their course in fashion design.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 26 now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 26.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, Chantal.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: What did you think of the talk then?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Hi, Hugo.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I thought it was good once I'd moved seats.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Oh, were the people beside you chatting or something?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: It wasn't that.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I went early so that I'd get a seat and not have to stand, but then this guy sat right in front of me, and he was so tall.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: It's hard to see through people's heads, isn't it?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Impossible.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Anyway, to answer your question, I thought it was really interesting.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Especially what the speaker said about the job market.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Me too.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: I mean we know we're going into a really competitive field, so it's obvious that we may struggle to get work.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: And we know we can't all have that dream job.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Yeah, but it looks like there's a whole range of areas of work that we hadn't even thought of.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Like fashion journalism for instance.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Yeah, I wasn't expecting so many career options.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Hmm, overall she had quite a strong message, didn't she?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: She did.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: She kept saying things like 'I know you all think this, but...' and then she'd tell us how it really is.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Perhaps she thinks students are a bit narrow-minded about the industry.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: It was a bit harsh though.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: We know it's a tough industry.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Yeah, and we're only first years after all.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: We've got a lot to learn.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Do you think our secondary-school education should have been more career focused?
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Well, we had numerous talks on careers, which was good, but none of them were very inspiring.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: They could have asked more people like today's speaker to talk to us.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: I agree.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: We were told about lots of different careers, just when we needed to be, but not by the experts who really know stuff.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: So, did today's talk influence your thoughts on what career you'd like to take up in the future?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Well, I promised myself that I'd go through this course and keep an open mind till the end.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: But I think it's better to pick an area of the industry now, and then aim to get better and better at it.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Well, I think we'll just have to differ on that issue.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: One thing's for certain though.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: From what she said, we'll be unpaid assistants in the industry for quite a long time.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I'm prepared for that, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Actually, I'm not going to accept that view.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Really?
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: But she knows it's the case, and everyone else says the same.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: That doesn't mean it has to be true for me.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: OK, well I hope you're right.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 27 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I thought the speaker's account of her first job was fascinating.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Yeah, she admitted she was lucky to get work being a personal dresser for a musician.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: She didn't even apply for the job, and there she was, getting paid to choose all his clothes.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: It must have felt amazing.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Though she said all she was looking for back then was experience, not financial reward.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Mmm.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: And then he was so mean telling her she was more interested in her own appearance than his.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: But - she did realize he was right about that, which really made me think.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I'm always considering my own clothes, but now I can see you should be focusing on your client.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: She obviously regretted losing the job.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Well, as she said, she should have hidden her negative feelings about him, but she didn't.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: It was really brave the way she picked herself up and took that job in retail.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Fancy working in a shop after that.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Yeah, well, she recommended we all do it at some point.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I guess as a designer, you'd get to find out some useful information.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Like how big or small the average shopper is.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: I think that's an issue for manufacturers, not designers.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: However, it would be useful to know if there's a gap in the market, you know.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: An item that no one's stocking, but that consumers are looking for.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Yeah, people don't give up searching.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: They also take things back to the store if they aren't right.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Imagine you worked in an expensive shop, and you found out the garments sold there were being returned because they fell apart in the wash.
audioscript.speaker: HUGO

audioscript.text: Yeah, it would be good to know that kind of thing.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: Yeah.
audioscript.speaker: CHANTAL

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What problem did Chantal have at the start of the talk?
options: Her view of the speaker was blocked.
She was unable to find an empty seat.
The students next to her were talking.

items.title: What were Hugo and Chantal surprised to hear about the job market?
options: It has become more competitive than it used to be.
There is more variety in it than they had realised.
Some areas of it are more exciting than others.

items.title: Hugo and Chantal agree that the speaker's message was
options: unfair to them at times.
hard for them to follow.
critical of the industry.

items.title: What do Hugo and Chantal criticise about their school careers advice?
options: when they received the advice
how much advice was given
who gave the advice

items.title: When discussing their future, Hugo and Chantal disagree on
options: which is the best career in fashion.
when to choose a career in fashion.
why they would like a career in fashion.

items.title: How does Hugo feel about being an unpaid assistant?
options: He is realistic about the practice.
He feels the practice is dishonest.
He thinks others want to change the practice.

body.title: Talk on jobs in fashion design
Which TWO mistakes did the speaker admit she made in her first job?
options: being dishonest to her employer
paying too much attention to how she looked
expecting to become well known
trying to earn a lot of money
openly disliking her client

body.title: Which TWO pieces of retail information do Hugo and Chantal agree would be useful?
options: the reasons people return fashion items
how much time people have to shop for clothes
fashion designs people want but can't find
the best time of year for fashion buying
the most popular fashion sizes

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear a zoology student giving a presentation on the process of moving wild elephants to a new reserve known as translocation.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For my presentation today, I want to tell you about how groups of elephants have been moved and settled in new reserves.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is known as translocation, and has been carried out in Malawi in Africa in recent years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The reason this is being done is because of overpopulation of elephants in some areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Overpopulation is a good problem to have, and not one we tend to hear about very often, in Malawi's Majete National Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The elephant population had been wiped out by poachers who killed the elephants for their ivory, but in 2003 the park was restocked.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And effective law enforcement was introduced.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since then, not a single elephant has been poached.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In this safe environment, the elephant population boomed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Breeding went so well that there were more elephants than the park could support.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This led to a number of problems.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Firstly, there was more competition for food, which meant that some elephants were suffering from hunger.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As there was a limit to the amount of food in the national park, some elephants began looking further afield.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Elephants were routinely knocking down fences around the park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Which then had to be repaired at a significant cost.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To solve this problem, the decision was made to move dozens of elephants from Majete National Park to Nkhotakota Wildlife Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where there were no elephants. But obviously, attempting to move significant numbers of elephants to a new home 300 kilometers away.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Is quite a challenge.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, how did this translocation process work in practice?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Elephants were moved in groups of between eight and 20, all belonging to one family.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Because relationships are very important to elephants, they all had to be moved at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A team of vets and park rangers flew over the park in helicopters, and targeted a group, which were rounded up, and directed to a designated open plain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The vets then used darts to immobilize the elephants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was a tricky maneuver, as they not only had to select the right dose of tranquilizer.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For different sized elephants, but they had to dart the elephants as they were running around.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This also had to be done as quickly as possible, so as to minimize the stress caused.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As soon as the elephants began to flop onto the ground, the team moved in to take care of them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To avoid the risk of suffocation, the team had to make sure none of the elephants were lying on their chests, because their lungs could be crushed in this position.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, all the elephants had to be placed on their sides.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One person stayed with each elephant, while they waited for the vets to do checks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was very important to keep an eye on their breathing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: If there were fewer than 6 breaths per minute, the elephant would need urgent medical attention.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Collars were fitted to the matriarch in each group, so their movements could be tracked in their new home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Measurements were taken of each elephant's tusks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Elephants with large tusks would be at greater risk from poachers, and also of their feet.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The elephants were then taken to a recovery area, before being loaded onto trucks and transported to their new home.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The elephants translocated to Nkhotakota settled in very well, and the project has generally been accepted to have been a huge success.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And not just for the elephants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Employment prospects have improved enormously, contributing to rising living standards for the whole community.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Poaching is no longer an issue, as former poachers are able to find more reliable sources of income.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, many of them volunteered to give up their weapons.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they were no longer of any use to them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: More than two dozen elephants have been born at Nkhotakota since relocation. With an area of more than 1,800 square kilometers, there's plenty of space for the elephant population to continue to grow.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Their presence is also helping to rebalance Nkhotakota's damaged ecosystem.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And providing a sustainable conservation model, which could be replicated in other parks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: All this has been a big draw for tourism, which contributes five times more than the illegal wildlife trade to GDP.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And this is mainly because of the elephants.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's also been a dramatic rise in interest from...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, how did this translocation process work in practice?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Elephants were moved in groups of between eight and 20, all belonging to one family.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Because relationships are very important to elephants, they all had to be moved at the same time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A team of vets and park rangers flew over the park in helicopters, and targeted a group, which were rounded up, and directed to a designated open plain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The vets then used darts to immobilize the elephants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was a tricky maneuver, as they not only had to select the right dose of tranquilizer.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For different sized elephants, but they had to dart the elephants as they were running around.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This also had to be done as quickly as possible, so as to minimize the stress caused.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As soon as the elephants began to flop onto the ground, the team moved in to take care of them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To avoid the risk of suffocation, the team had to make sure none of the elephants were lying on their chests, because their lungs could be crushed in this position.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So, all the elephants had to be placed on their sides.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One person stayed with each elephant, while they waited for the vets to do checks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was very important to keep an eye on their breathing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: If there were fewer than 6 breaths per minute, the elephant would need urgent medical attention.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Collars were fitted to the matriarch in each group, so their movements could be tracked in their new home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Measurements were taken of each elephant's tusks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Elephants with large tusks would be at greater risk from poachers, and also of their feet.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The elephants were then taken to a recovery area, before being loaded onto trucks and transported to their new home.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The elephants translocated to Nkhotakota settled in very well, and the project has generally been accepted to have been a huge success.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And not just for the elephants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Employment prospects have improved enormously, contributing to rising living standards for the whole community.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Poaching is no longer an issue, as former poachers are able to find more reliable sources of income.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, many of them volunteered to give up their weapons.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As they were no longer of any use to them.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: More than two dozen elephants have been born at Nkhotakota since relocation. With an area of more than 1,800 square kilometers, there's plenty of space for the elephant population to continue to grow.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Their presence is also helping to rebalance Nkhotakota's damaged ecosystem.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And providing a sustainable conservation model, which could be replicated in other parks.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: All this has been a big draw for tourism, which contributes five times more than the illegal wildlife trade to GDP.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And this is mainly because of the elephants.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There's also been a dramatic rise in interest from...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Reasons for overpopulation at Majete National Park

items: greater competition, causing hunger for elephants
items.title: Problems caused by elephant overpopulation

elephants' <input> had to be monitored constantly

an increase in <input> as a contributor to GDP
items.title: Advantages of translocation at Nkhotakota Wildlife Park
